title: Hearing and Seeing
speaker: Mr. Isaiah Couch
source_files: 02-25-24 -PM- Hearing and Seeing - Job 42_1-6 - Mr. Isaiah Couch.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Job's Crisis and God's Answer
subpoints: Job suffered tremendous loss — family, health, wealth — and throughout the book asked 'Why?' and 'Where are you?'
Job 7:11-16: Job's complaint — he wished he had never been born; bitter questions about God's purpose
Job 19:25-27: the turning point — 'I know that my Redeemer lives' — even in suffering Job confessed faith in a coming Deliverer
God's answer came not in explanation but in presence — the voice from the whirlwind (Job 38-41)

outline.title: 1. Recognition: Creature Acknowledges Creator (v.1)
subpoints: 'I know that you can do everything, and that no purpose of yours can be withheld from you' (v.2)
The framework: creator/creature — Job moves from demanding an explanation to acknowledging that God owes him none
Isaiah 55:8-9: 'My thoughts are not your thoughts, nor are your ways my ways' — the Creator-creature distinction is the foundation of Job's recognition
This is not resignation but revelation — Job has seen something of who God actually is

outline.title: 2. Recalling: Job Confronted with His Own Words (vv.3-4)
subpoints: God had asked from the whirlwind: 'Who is this who darkens counsel by words without knowledge?' (Job 38:2)
Job recalls and applies this to himself: 'I have uttered what I did not understand, things too wonderful for me, which I did not know'
The humbling moment: Job had spoken, but he had spoken beyond his knowledge — he had offered opinions about God's purposes that he was not equipped to make
'The moon is always round' — God is always good and always sovereign even when we cannot see his full plan from our vantage point

outline.title: 3. Repentance: Seeing Produces Contrition (vv.5-6)
subpoints: 'I have heard of you by the hearing of the ear, but now my eye sees you. Therefore I abhor myself and repent in dust and ashes' (vv.5-6)
The difference between secondhand knowledge and firsthand encounter — hearing about God versus seeing him
Isaiah 6:5 parallel: when Isaiah saw the Lord, his first response was 'Woe is me! For I am undone!' — a vision of God produces self-knowledge
2 Corinthians 7:10-11: this is godly sorrow that leads to repentance — not worldly sorrow (self-pity) but genuine contrition before God

outline.title: Application: Redeemer/Redeemed
subpoints: Job 19:25-27: 'I know that my Redeemer lives, and he shall stand at last on the earth; and after my skin is destroyed, this I know, that in my flesh I shall see God'
The book of Job is not primarily a book about suffering; it is a book about who God is — the Creator who is also Redeemer
Romans 7:24 — Paul's 'Wretched man that I am! Who will deliver me?' is answered: the Redeemer who lives
The Christian response to suffering follows Job's pattern: lament honestly, encounter God's presence, move from hearing to seeing, find the Redeemer at the center

summary: Mr. Isaiah Couch preaches on Job 42:1-6 — Job's final response to God's answer from the whirlwind. Three movements are traced: recognition (Job acknowledges God's absolute sovereignty and his own creature-hood), recalling (Job hears God's original question from Job 38 applied to himself — he had spoken beyond his knowledge), and repentance (seeing God produces contrition: 'I abhor myself and repent in dust and ashes'). The distinction between secondhand knowledge (hearing about God) and firsthand encounter (seeing him) is central. The framework of creator/creature and redeemer/redeemed holds the book together: Job found not an explanation for his suffering but the Redeemer himself.
transcript:
## Introduction: Job's Long Question

The book of Job is one of the most difficult and most honest books in all of Scripture. Job was a righteous man — the text says so explicitly — and he suffered tremendously. He lost his children, his wealth, his health. And throughout the book, he asks the question that all suffering people ask: *Why? Where are you? What are you doing?*

In Job 7:11-16, we hear his complaint at its rawest: he wishes he had never been born. He questions the purpose of his existence. He cries out with the bitterness that many of us have felt but been afraid to voice.

But even in that darkness, Job 19 gives us one of the most remarkable statements in the entire Old Testament: *I know that my Redeemer lives, and he shall stand at last on the earth; and after my skin is destroyed, this I know, that in my flesh I shall see God, whom I shall see for myself.* In the middle of his suffering, Job confessed faith in a living Redeemer. He did not know who that Redeemer was by name. But he knew he was coming.

God's answer, when it came, was not an explanation. It was a presence. From Job 38 to 41, God speaks from the whirlwind — not to explain Job's suffering but to reveal himself. *Where were you when I laid the foundations of the earth?* He asks question after question that Job cannot answer, not to humiliate him but to situate him — to help him understand who he is and who God is.

Chapter 42 is Job's response.

## 1. Recognition (vv. 1-2)

*Then Job answered the LORD and said: I know that you can do everything, and that no purpose of yours can be withheld from you.*

This is recognition. Job moves from demanding an explanation to acknowledging that God owes him none. This is the creator-creature distinction made personal and concrete. Isaiah 55:8-9: *My thoughts are not your thoughts, nor are your ways my ways, says the LORD. For as the heavens are higher than the earth, so are my ways higher than your ways, and my thoughts than your thoughts.*

Job is not giving up. He is not resigning himself to a cold, arbitrary universe. He is recognizing that the God he is dealing with is the Creator of all things — and that the Creator does not answer to the creature. He acts according to his own purposes, which are good, even when they are not visible.

There is an illustration I find helpful: the moon is always round. Every night, whether we see a crescent or a half-moon or no moon at all, the moon itself is always round. Our vantage point changes what we see, but the moon does not change. God is always good, always sovereign, always working his purposes — even when we can only see a crescent.

## 2. Recalling (vv.3-4)

*You asked, 'Who is this who hides counsel without knowledge?' Therefore I have uttered what I did not understand, things too wonderful for me, which I did not know.*

Job is recalling God's opening question from the whirlwind in Job 38:2: *Who is this who darkens counsel by words without knowledge?* And he is applying it to himself. The humbling has happened. Job had spoken. He had complained, argued, demanded. He had offered opinions about God's purposes and God's justice and God's absence. And now, having heard from God, he realizes: he was speaking beyond his knowledge.

This is not a retraction of everything Job said. Job's laments were honest, and God honored them. But there was a point at which honest lament crossed into something else — into accusations about God's character, into demands that God explain himself, into the assumption that Job's perspective on his own situation was accurate. And it was not.

*Things too wonderful for me, which I did not know.* The humility here is not self-abasement. It is accurate self-knowledge. Job was not equipped to judge God's purposes in his suffering, because God's purposes ran deeper than Job's suffering. The story was bigger than he could see.

## 3. Repentance (vv.5-6)

*I have heard of you by the hearing of the ear, but now my eye sees you. Therefore I abhor myself and repent in dust and ashes.*

This is the hinge of the book. And it turns on a distinction that is crucial: the difference between hearing and seeing.

Job had known about God. He was a righteous man — he offered sacrifices, he prayed, he was blameless in his generation. He had *heard* of God. But there is a difference between secondhand knowledge and firsthand encounter.

The parallel in Isaiah 6 is exact. When Isaiah saw the Lord, high and lifted up, his first response was: *Woe is me! For I am undone! Because I am a man of unclean lips.* A genuine vision of God does not produce pride or argument. It produces self-knowledge. It shows us what we are in light of who he is.

Job saw. And what he saw produced repentance. *I abhor myself and repent in dust and ashes.* 2 Corinthians 7:10-11: *Godly grief produces a repentance that leads to salvation without regret, whereas worldly grief produces death.* Job's grief, in the end, was godly grief. It moved outward and upward — toward God — rather than inward in self-pity.

This is what an encounter with God does. It does not leave us where we were.

## Application: The Redeemer at the Center

The book of Job is not primarily a book about suffering. It is a book about who God is. The suffering is the context; God is the subject.

And the God who is revealed is not merely Creator but Redeemer. Job knew it before he could see it clearly: *I know that my Redeemer lives.* He was speaking of Christ, though he did not yet know the name. The one who would stand at last on the earth. The one in whose flesh Job would one day see God.

Paul in Romans 7:24: *Wretched man that I am! Who will deliver me from this body of death?* And the answer: *Thanks be to God through Jesus Christ our Lord.* Paul's lament has an answer. Job's suffering has a Redeemer.

When you are in the midst of suffering — when you have heard but not yet seen, when you are in the complaint of Job 7 and not yet at the recognition of Job 42 — the path is the same. Be honest. Bring your lament before God. And look for the Redeemer, who is alive, who stands, who will at last bring you to see him face to face.

Father, we thank you for this difficult and beautiful book. Thank you that Job's suffering was not meaningless — it revealed you. We pray that in our own suffering we would follow Job's path: from complaint to recognition, from hearing to seeing, from argument to repentance. Keep us looking for our living Redeemer. In Jesus' name, amen.
